title: Chocolate on Silk
stainChemistry: Chocolate is a triple-threat composite: cocoa butter (fat), cocoa solids (tannin-like polyphenols), and milk proteins (if milk chocolate). Chocolate combines all three bonding mechanisms — fat penetrates hydrophobically, tannins form hydrogen bonds, and protein bonds through peptide bridges. On silk, the cocoa butter melts at body temperature and carries the tannin and protein components deep into the fiber. Protein fiber is especially vulnerable — the milk protein component creates like-on-like bonding. Heat is the enemy — it melts the cocoa butter further, spreading the stain, while denaturing the protein component permanently.
whyThisWorks: We're attacking chocolate with the chemistry that matches its bonding mechanism. Cold water prevents thermal denaturation. NSD's surfactants surround and lift the protein molecules without setting them. On silk, we can't use enzymes — they'd digest the fiber along with the stain. So we rely on NSD's surfactant action for longer dwell times, followed by reducing bleach to break any color component. It's gentler, slower, but it works without damaging the fiber. The sequence matters — protein must be treated before any heat exposure, and all chemistry must be flushed and neutralized to prevent residual damage.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Spray bottle with distilled water, white cotton towels
spottingProtocol.instruction: Flush with cold distilled water. Cold water only — heat denatures protein and cross-links it to the fiber, making the stain permanent. Spray and blot with a clean white towel. You will see color and residue transfer to the towel. Keep flushing until the runoff is mostly clear.

spottingProtocol.technique: Surfactant lift of protein and lipid components
spottingProtocol.equipment: Spray bottle or dropper, white cotton pads, soft brush
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply diluted NSD (1 teaspoon per cup of cold water) to the stain. Chocolate contains proteins and fats that bond to fiber through hydrogen bonds and hydrophobic interactions. NSD's surfactants surround these molecules and lift them free. Blot gently — no agitation on silk. You'll see residue transferring to the towel. Repeat until fresh towel sections show minimal pickup.
spottingProtocol.suggestedProducts: Mulsolite®, Woolite Delicates, or neutral lube

spottingProtocol.agent: Extended NSD treatment — no enzymes safe on silk
spottingProtocol.technique: Repeated surfactant lift — protect protein fiber
spottingProtocol.instruction: CRITICAL: Do NOT use enzyme cleaners on silk — protease enzymes digest protein fibers. The enzyme can't tell the difference between chocolate protein and silk protein. Instead, repeat NSD applications with fresh solution. Gentle blotting after each dwell. Each pass should lift more residue.

spottingProtocol.agent: Apply amyl acetate or POG — dissolve fat/lipid component
spottingProtocol.instruction: Chocolate has a significant fat component (lipids and oils) that NSD alone may not fully emulsify. Amyl acetate or a POG (paint-oil-grease) spotter dissolves the remaining lipid residue. Apply sparingly — a few drops on the stain. The fat component will soften and transfer to your blotting towel. One application is usually sufficient. Flush with cold water after.
spottingProtocol.suggestedProducts: Amyl acetate, Afta® POG spotter, Carbona Stain Devils #5

spottingProtocol.technique: Thorough rinse — remove all chemical residue
spottingProtocol.instruction: Flush thoroughly with cold distilled water. Three to four passes, blotting between each. You're removing all traces of NSD, enzyme, and dissolved stain. Residual chemistry left behind can yellow or damage the silk over time. The towel should show no color transfer by the last flush.

Apply diluted acetic acid (28% GAA at 1:10, or white vinegar at 1:3 with cold water) to restore pH balance. This is critical on protein fiber — alkaline residue opens the scale structure and can cause permanent damage. Spray, let sit, blot. One final cold water flush and blot dry.

Lay flat on a clean towel — never hang-dry or heat-dry protein fiber. No heat until the stain is confirmed gone. Heat on residual protein permanently sets it through denaturation.  Check under good light once dry.

homeSolutions: Dish soap + cold water: Mix 1 tsp dish soap in 1 cup cold water. Apply to stain, let sit 5 minutes, blot. The surfactants lift protein without setting it. Repeat as needed.
White vinegar solution: Mix 1 part white vinegar to 2 parts cold water. Apply, let sit 5 minutes, blot. The acidity helps break protein bonds.
materialWarnings: NEVER use hot water — heat denatures protein and permanently cross-links it to silk. Cold to cool water only (≤30°C/86°F).
NEVER use alcohol on protein stains — alcohol dehydrates and permanently fixes protein through cross-linking.
NEVER use protease enzymes (OxiClean, Biz, enzyme cleaners) on silk — enzymes digest protein fiber. The enzyme cannot distinguish between stain protein and fiber protein.
NEVER use chlorine bleach on silk — it damages fiber structure and can permanently set protein stains.
NEVER rub or agitate silk while wet — friction + moisture = fiber damage. Blot and press only.
NEVER use alkaline cleaners (ammonia, sodium hydroxide, strong detergents) — alkalinity damages protein fiber and accelerates stain bonding.
ALWAYS test any spotter on a hidden area of silk first — dyes and finishes vary.
ALWAYS use distilled water for flushes — tap water minerals can interact with spotters and leave residue.
escalation: If the stain persists after this protocol: repeat the enzyme step with extended dwell time. If the stain is set by heat or age, professional treatment with concentrated agents and spotting board equipment is the next step. For valuable or irreplaceable items, professional assessment is always worth the cost — a skilled spotter has chemistry and equipment that home methods can't match.

professional.name: Mulsolite® (NSD)
professional.note: Industry-standard neutral detergent. Safe on all fibers. Primary protein stain lifter.

professional.name: GAA (28% Acetic Acid)
professional.note: Professional-grade pH neutralizer. Essential for post-treatment fiber balance.

household.name: Dawn Dish Soap
household.note: Mild surfactant. Mix 1 tsp per cup of cold water for protein stain lifting.

household.name: White Vinegar (5% Acetic Acid)
household.note: Household acid for pH neutralization. Dissolves mineral deposits, neutralizes ammonia.

household.name: Hydrogen Peroxide (3%)
household.note: Drugstore-strength oxidizer. Test on hidden area first on colored fabric.

customerExplanation: Chocolate is a protein-based stain that bonds to silk through chemical attraction between the protein and the fiber surface. We treated it with a gentle detergent to surround and lift the protein, using careful chemistry appropriate for this delicate surface. The key was keeping temperature low — heat permanently sets protein stains.
commonMistakes: Using hot water — heat denatures protein and permanently sets the stain into silk. The protein coagulates and bonds irreversibly — there's no recovery from this.
Rubbing the stain — especially dangerous on silk which felts with friction and moisture. Blot and press only.
Using enzyme cleaners on silk — protease enzymes digest protein fiber just like they digest the stain. The fiber weakens and eventually dissolves.
scienceNote: Protein stains bond to surfaces through hydrogen bonds between peptide groups (-C=O and -N-H) in the protein and similar groups in the fiber. These are individually weak bonds, but proteins form hundreds of them simultaneously — creating a cumulative adhesion that's surprisingly strong. Heat accelerates denaturation, which unfolds the protein chain and exposes more bonding sites, dramatically increasing adhesion. This is why hot water is the critical mistake with protein stains: it doesn't just fail to help — it actively makes the stain harder to remove.
callPro: If the stain is more than a few weeks old, has been heat-set, or is on a valuable/irreplaceable item: call a professional. Silk requires specialized handling — a professional dry cleaner with experience in delicate fiber spotting has the equipment (spotting board, steam control) and chemistry (professional-grade reducing bleach, pH-buffered formulas) to treat this safely. If you've tried this protocol and the stain is faint but persistent, a pro can attempt additional chemistry without the risk of further damage. Explain what products you've already used — that helps them avoid redundant or conflicting treatments.
difficulty_factors: Protein fiber — enzyme restriction limits treatment options
Delicate handling required — fiber damage risk
solventNote: This protocol uses wet-side spotting only. For PERC/HC shops: level with appropriate solvent before machine processing. For wet clean shops: proceed with gentle wet clean cycle after treatment.
